title: Natural Selection in the Wake of Catastrophe
authors: Jesse Young Lin, Omer Granek, Joshua Sodicoff, Seppe Kuehn, David Pincus, Vincenzo Vitelli
abstract: Living organisms, from bacteria to humans, are more likely to survive if their traits enhance fitness. In populations well adapted to their environmental niches, natural selection proceeds via rarely beneficial mutations. But when a catastrophe wipes out niche diversity, sudden adaptation often follows. Here, we present a data-validated theory of natural selection in the wake of catastrophe and unveil a simple law that emerges during recovery: the mean fitness relaxes inversely with time, with a prefactor proportional to the number of traits coupled to the post-catastrophe environment. We put our approach to test using experimental fitness landscapes measured following antibiotic administration to E. coli. The resulting mean trait adaptation is not described by gradient ascent on a fitness landscape, instead it follows an algorithm known as Levenberg-Marquardt optimization. Near fitness peaks, evolutionary trajectories are biased against greediness - from an optimization perspective, post-catastrophic selection is optimistic.
summary: This paper develops a theory showing that adaptation after an environmental catastrophe follows predictable, slow dynamics characterized by mean fitness decaying inversely with time. By analyzing this decay rate using mathematical tools from statistical physics, the authors propose that the process acts as a probe to measure the effective dimensionality of the underlying phenotypic space.
main_problem: The study investigates the dynamics of natural selection following a catastrophic environmental event, modeling adaptation as an optimization flow on a fitness landscape.
main_result: Post-catastrophic mean fitness decay follows a universal inverse-time law ($\langle D(t) angle \propto 1/t$), suggesting that recovery probes the dimensionality ($n/Q$) of the underlying phenotypic space.
method: The authors use theoretical derivations based on the replicator equation, applying Laplace's method for asymptotic analysis, and validating results using kinetic fitness assays derived from experimental data.
model_or_system: The system models trait evolution $z$ in populations (like E. coli) recovering from stress. The dynamics are governed by selection acting on standing variation within a multi-dimensional fitness landscape $r(z)$.
key_observables: Mean fitness cost decay $\langle D(t) angle$, specific fitness $c(t)$, and the ratio of phenotypic dimension to curvature ($n/Q$).
important_parameters: Time scale $t$, number of traits $n$, local curvature parameter $Q$, and crossover time $	au_c$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes selection on standing variation (SSV) is dominant, and that the system can be approximated by a continuum near the fitness optimum.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate catastrophe types, the analogy to calorimetry for dimensionality measurement, and comparisons between theoretical trajectories (e.g., gradient ascent vs. Levenberg-Marquardt optimization) on a 2D landscape.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the mathematical framework using replicator dynamics, derives the universal $1/t$ decay law, links this to statistical mechanics concepts like equipartition, and validates the dimensionality measurement ($n/Q$) using multiple experimental assays (E. coli) and theoretical tools (Laplace's method).
why_interesting: The mathematical framework for analyzing relaxation dynamics in complex systems, particularly the use of asymptotic expansions (Laplace's method) to extract fundamental geometric parameters ($n/Q$), shares conceptual links with calculating correlation functions or partition functions in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Catastrophes turn standing variation into a dynamical probe of fitness-landscape geometry. (a) Ecosystem collapse after acute stress crosses a tipping point, (b) mutagenic stress causing uncontrolled mutagenesis, and (c) Inferring phenotypic fitness landscape geometry from sequencing data through selection on standing variation time traces. A power law regime in the mean fitness cost decay reveals the number of fitness-relevant traits. Initial recovery is dominated by selection on the fitness landscape defined by the trait-dependent rate r(z1, z2). Replicator dynamics, Eq. FIG. 2. Static fitness assays in E. coli measure the phenotypic dimension relevant to evolutionary response. Two complementary assays measure n/Q, where n is the number of independent traits under selection and Q describes the curvature of the fitness peak. Static assays screen a whole library of variants at once, giving a distribution of fitness costs relative to the best variant; the cumulative fraction of variants within a rescaled fitness cost ρ = D/Dmax is predicted to scale as Σ(ρ) ∼ρn/Q. Kinetic assays track a recovering population over time, giving the mean remaining fitness cost ⟨D(t)⟩which is predicted to decay as (n/Q)/t (Eq. (5); the 1/t slope is universal). The two are...

FIG. 3. Selection on standing variation follows curvature-sensitive trajectories on fitness landscapes. (a) Gradient ascent, Newton’s method, and Levenberg-Marquardt dynamics on the landscape r(z1, z2) = 10 −z2 1 −4z2 2 + 0.1(z2 1 + z2 2)2, and the Levenberg-Marquardt dynamics which interpolates between them. The Levenberg-Marquardt dynamics adaptively selects between the steepest ascent (gradient, blue) and a curvature-sensitive ascent (Newton, purple), with directions shown by arrows. While gradient flow is preferable on the steep fitness slopes, Newton’s method is preferable near the peak where some directions may be shallow.
